# TCP in-flight visualizer

> An interactive browser-based simulator for **TCP behavior on a single bulk transfer**. Visualizes the sender, receiver,
> kernel buffers on both ends, the bottleneck router queue between them, and the network in between. Includes a
> programmable JavaScript API (`window.SimAgent`) so agents can configure runs, read structured results, and watch the
> same animation a human would see.

Production is deployed at `https://tcp.overshoot.ai`. On Vercel, `/` is rewritten directly to
`tcp-inflight.html`, so `window.SimAgent` is on the top window. In local static serving, `index.html`
is a thin iframe wrapper that loads `tcp-inflight.html`; agents driving the local root should target
the iframe's `contentWindow`. Agents loading `tcp-inflight.html` directly always get
`window.SimAgent` on the top window.

---

## What this is

TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) is the protocol that does almost all bulk data transfer on the internet â€” file
uploads, HTTP requests, video streaming control planes, etc. It's reliable (retransmits lost packets), ordered, and
**congestion-controlled** (adapts its send rate to network conditions). The exact way TCP adapts â€” its *congestion
control algorithm* â€” has huge implications for real-world throughput, latency, and how a single connection behaves on
shared infrastructure.

This site simulates a single TCP transfer (one client â†’ one server; the blog presets send a 5 MB body) over a network
with a configurable bottleneck. You can watch every byte of the request leave the sender, traverse the network, queue
at the bottleneck, and arrive at the receiver. The simulator models **TCP Cubic** (the default congestion control in Linux
since ~2008), with slow-start, Hystart, fast retransmit, fast recovery, and RTO, plus **TCP BBR** (Google's 2016
model-based controller). It also models **bottleneck queue dynamics** â€” packets fill the queue, queue overflow causes
drops, drops trigger retransmits â€” and **flow control** via finite kernel buffers.

The intended audience is anyone who wants to *see* how TCP works under specific conditions: developers debugging
transfer performance, students learning networking, agents running experiments. The visualization is the teaching tool;
the agent API makes it possible to run experiments programmatically.

## What the screen shows

**Top region** (~65% of canvas):

- **Left column â€” Sender**:
  - *Body grid* on top: a grid of cells, one per MSS-sized packet of the payload. Cells are bright cyan when "buffered"
    (data exists on the sender ready to go), blue when "in flight" (this packet has been sent but not yet
    acknowledged), dim when "drained" (sent and acknowledged).
  - *send-buffer bucket (wmem)* below: visualizes the kernel send buffer (`tcp_wmem`). Cells are packet-sized slots. Filled cells = bytes
    the app has written to the kernel that haven't been ACKed yet.
- **Middle column â€” HUD**: live numbers â€” goodput (KB/ms and Mbps), bound-by, request time, completion %, and a dense
  stats grid below with cwnd, in-flight bytes, queue depth, retransmits, recv window, etc.
- **Right column â€” Receiver**: mirror of the sender's body grid and recv-buffer bucket (rmem). Cells turn bright cyan as packets
  arrive.

**Bottom region** (~35% of canvas, full canvas width):

- **Network band**: a horizontal strip showing packets in transit. Split in two legs (pre-queue, post-queue) by the
  bottleneck router buffer in the middle. Data packets (blue dots) flow left-to-right along the top half; ACKs (green
  dots) flow right-to-left along the bottom half. Packet Y position is random (just for visual separation â€” TCP is a
  single byte stream physically).
- **Router buffer** (bottleneck): a vertical bucket in the middle of the band. Cells = MSS-sized slots; the bucket's
  height scales with the configured `queue` size. When a packet arrives at a full router buffer, it gets *dropped* â€”
  visualized as a red dot falling out the bottom of the bucket (gravity-like animation in real time).

**Sidebar** (right of canvas):

- Preset selector + scenario card (story, what to watch, expected outcome)
- Knobs (sliders): RTT, cwnd, MSS, payload, sim speed, router read/write rates, router buffer, loss %, and the congestion-control
  options.
- Collapsible "Buffers" section: router read/write rates, send buffer (wmem), app write rate, receive buffer (rmem), app read rate.
- Start / Pause / Restart controls.
- Glossary: clickable terms (RTT, BDP, cwnd, Hystart, fast retransmit, etc.) with definitions. The canvas components
  themselves are also click targets (body grids, wmem/rmem buckets, router queue bucket, network band), as are legend
  items, and glossary/preset prose cross-links every known term it mentions.

**Mobile layout**:

- Portrait phones show a rotate-to-landscape prompt.
- Landscape phones prioritize the visualization: dense HUD stats are hidden, the headline keeps request time,
  bottleneck, and completion visible, and compact Start/Pause/Restart controls sit in the middle of the canvas.
- The sidebar knobs and legend are hidden by default on mobile landscape. `Knobs` and `Legend` buttons open them as
  overlays; opening one closes the other.

## Key TCP concepts you'll see in action

Anchored to what each one looks like on screen:

- **RTT** (round-trip time, in ms): how long a packet plus its ACK takes to traverse the network. Each packet takes
  RTT/2 across the band; its ACK takes another RTT/2 back. Knob: `RTT (ms)`.
- **MSS** (maximum segment size, bytes): how big each packet is (TCP's formal term for its data unit: segment). Typically ~1300â€“1500 bytes. Knob: `Packet size (MSS)`.
- **cwnd** (congestion window, in packets): a per-connection cap on the number of packets the sender may have
  *unacknowledged on the wire*. The goodput a single TCP flow can sustain is approximately `cwnd Ã— MSS / RTT`. Knob:
  `cwnd (pkts)`.
- **BDP** (bandwidth-delay product): `link_bw Ã— RTT / 8` in bytes â€” the "natural pipe size" of the path. Cwnd should
  reach BDP for TCP to fill the pipe. Derived from knobs.
- **slow-start**: TCP's startup phase. Cwnd starts small (typically 10 packets) and doubles every RTT until something
  stops it (loss, Hystart, or `ssthresh`). Animation: cwnd ramps up exponentially.
- **Hystart**: an algorithm that exits slow-start *before* cwnd causes a loss by detecting queueing (the simulator's
  Hystart fires when cwnd Ã— MSS > BDP). Animation: after Hystart fires, cwnd stops doubling and grows linearly.
- **Cubic**: the modern congestion control algorithm. On loss, cwnd is cut multiplicatively (Ã—0.7 in Cubic). It then
  regrows along a cubic curve toward the prior peak. Default since Linux 2.6.19.
- **BBR**: Google's 2016 model-based congestion control. Instead of reacting to packet loss, it continuously estimates
  two physical quantities â€” `BtlBw` (max delivery rate over the last ~10 RTTs) and `RTprop` (min RTT over the last
  ~10 sec). The sender paces packets at `BtlBw Ã— pacing_gain` with cwnd capped at `BtlBw Ã— RTprop Ã— cwnd_gain`
  (~2Ã— BDP). State machine: STARTUP doubles per RTT until delivery rate plateaus, DRAIN drains the queue STARTUP
  built, PROBE_BW runs an 8-phase pacing cycle `[1.25, 0.75, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1]` to keep probing for more bandwidth,
  PROBE_RTT briefly drops cwnd to 4 packets every 10 sec to re-measure RTprop. Largely ignores packet loss â€” designed for
  lossy or shallow-buffered paths where Cubic collapses.
- **Fast retransmit**: when 3 dup-ACKs arrive (signal that a packet is missing), the sender retransmits without waiting
  for an RTO timeout.
- **Fast recovery**: the cwnd reduction (Ã—0.7) is mild and recovery proceeds via cubic regrowth; cwnd doesn't crash to
  the one-packet RTO loss window unless recovery fails.
- **RTO** (retransmission timeout): the catastrophic loss path. Cwnd resets to a one-packet loss window and
  slow-start restarts. Reserved for serious problems.
- **Router read / write**: router read is the ingress rate into the bottleneck queue; router write is the router's
  egress capacity. The actual queue drain is `min(linkBwMbps, routerWriteMbps)`. Queue builds when router read exceeds
  that effective drain.
- **Router buffer (bottleneck queue)**: the buffer at the path's slowest link. Packets queue here when router read >
  effective drain. If it fills, packets drop. Knob: `Router (KB)`.
- **send / receive buffers (wmem / rmem)**: the sender's and receiver's kernel TCP buffers. wmem holds bytes from the app's write call until
  ACKed (kernel needs them in case of retransmit). rmem holds arrived bytes until the receiver app reads them. The
  receiver advertises `rwnd = rmem âˆ’ rmem_used` to the sender; sender's effective window is `min(cwnd, rwnd)`. Knobs in
  the "Buffers" section.

## Knobs (full reference)

All knobs are settable via `SimAgent.configure({...})` or via the sliders in the sidebar.

| Knob | API param | Range | What it does |
|---|---|---|---|
| RTT (ms) | `rtt` | 1â€“1000 | Round-trip time. Packets take RTT/2 across the band. |
| cwnd (pkts) | `cwnd` | 1â€“65536 | Hard cap on unacked segments. Used directly in `custom` cc mode; auto-managed in `cubic`/`bbr`. |
| Init cwnd (pkts) | `initialCwndSeg` | 1â€“65536 | Cwnd at the start of a cubic or BBR run (RFC 6928 default is 10). |
| Cubic pacing | `cubicPacing` | boolean | When true, Cubic keeps its cwnd/loss logic but spaces sends at `cwnd_bytes / RTT Ã— cubicPacingGain`. |
| Pacing gain | `cubicPacingGain` | 0.5â€“3 | Cubic pacer gain. `1.0` spreads one cwnd over one RTT; values above 1 probe more aggressively. |
| Packet size (MSS) | `mss` | 500â€“9000 | Max data bytes per packet. |
| Payload (MB) | `payloadMb` | 0.1â€“500 | Total request size. |
| Sim speed | `simSpeed` | 0â€“1 | Wall-clock to sim-clock ratio (log scale). 0.02 = 50Ã— slow-mo for watching. |
| Link bw (Mbps) | `linkBwMbps` | 1â€“100000 | Path capacity available to this flow. Default 1000 Mbps in blog presets. |
| router read (Mbps) | `routerReadMbps` | 1â€“100000 | Ingress rate into the bottleneck router queue. Default 10000 Mbps in blog presets. |
| router write (Mbps) | `routerWriteMbps` | 1â€“100000 | Egress capacity out of the router queue. Effective drain is `min(linkBwMbps, routerWriteMbps)`. Default 1000 Mbps in blog presets. |
| Router (KB) | `queueKB` | 4â€“16384 | Bottleneck buffer size (log-scale slider). |
| Loss (%) | `lossPct` | 0â€“20 | Background per-packet random loss (independent of queue overflow). |
| send buf (KB) | `wmemKB` | 4â€“32768 | Sender kernel send buffer (wmem). |
| recv buf (KB) | `rmemKB` | 4â€“32768 | Receiver kernel recv buffer (rmem). |
| app write (Mbps) | `appWriteMbps` | 0â€“10000 | Appâ†’wmem write rate. 0 = writes nothing. |
| app read (Mbps) | `appReadMbps` | 0â€“10000 | rmemâ†’app drain rate. 0 = reads nothing. |
| Mode | `mode` | tcp \| udp | UDP disables ACKs, retransmits, flow control. |
| Handshake | `handshake` | true \| false | TCP 3-way handshake before data â€” shown as cold (true) vs warm (false) in the UI. When true (cold), the first data segment leaves at exactly t = RTT (SYN out, SYN-ACK back; the final ACK rides with the first data flight). Default false (warm/reused connection). Ignored in UDP mode. |
| CC algo | `ccMode` | custom \| cubic \| bbr | "custom" keeps cwnd at the knob value; "cubic" runs RFC 8312; "bbr" runs Google's 2016 BBR. |

### Presets

Three scenario presets, matching the blog/video progression. A preset sets every model knob plus the congestion-control
mode and initial cwnd. Apply via `SimAgent.applyPreset(name)`:

| Preset key | Scenario | The lesson |
|---|---|---|
| `cold-iw10` | Cold TCP, IW10 | Fresh TCP connection, default 10-packet initial window. 5 MB in ~353 ms in the sim. |
| `warm-iw10` | Warm TCP, IW10 | Reused TCP connection, default 10-packet initial window. 5 MB in ~326 ms in the sim. |
| `warm-iw3500-rwnd-warm` | Warm TCP, IW3500 + rwnd warm | Reused connection, aggressive initial window, receive window opened by a prior large upload. 5 MB in ~78 ms in the sim. |

Preset links are supported with query params:

```text
https://tcp.overshoot.ai/?preset=cold-iw10
https://tcp.overshoot.ai/?preset=warm-iw10
https://tcp.overshoot.ai/?preset=warm-iw3500-rwnd-warm

tcp-inflight.html?preset=cold-iw10
tcp-inflight.html?preset=warm-iw10
tcp-inflight.html?preset=warm-iw3500-rwnd-warm&autoplay=1
```

Supported startup params:

| Param | Values | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `preset` or `p` | preset key | Loads that preset; unknown keys fall back to `cold-iw10`. |
| `cc` or `ccMode` | `custom` \| `cubic` \| `bbr` | Overrides the preset congestion-control mode. |
| `cubicPacing`, `paceCubic`, or `pacing` | `1`/`0`, `true`/`false`, `on`/`off` | Enables/disables optional Cubic sender pacing. |
| `cubicPacingGain`, `pacingGain`, or `paceGain` | number | Sets Cubic pacer gain. |
| `mode` | `tcp` \| `udp` | Overrides transport mode. |
| `handshake` | `cold`/`warm`, `true`/`false`, `1`/`0` | Sets TCP handshake visualization. |
| `speed` or `simSpeed` | 0â€“1 | Sets visual playback speed. |
| `autoplay` or `start` | `1`, `true`, `yes`, `on` | Starts the visual simulation on load. |

## Agent API: `window.SimAgent`

Loaded on the page. Use it from page-world JavaScript (Playwright's `page.evaluate`, devtools console, browser-extension
injected scripts, or similar). From within an iframe-wrapper page, access via
`document.querySelector('iframe').contentWindow.SimAgent`.

### Methods

```js
// --- configure ---
SimAgent.applyPreset(name)                   // preset key from the table above â€” resets
SimAgent.configure(params)                   // partial config, see knobs table for keys
SimAgent.setMode("tcp" | "udp")
SimAgent.setCcMode("custom" | "cubic" | "bbr")

// --- control ---
SimAgent.reset()
SimAgent.start()                             // begin RAF loop (visual playback)
SimAgent.pause()
SimAgent.step(ms)                            // advance N sim-ms synchronously
SimAgent.isRunning()                         // bool

// --- run-to-completion (both deterministic and visual) ---
await SimAgent.runToCompletion({ visual, maxSimMs })
  // visual: false (default) â€” tight loop, returns in ms wall-time, no animation
  // visual: true            â€” RAF loop, animates at current simSpeed, resolves on finish
  // maxSimMs: cap on sim time (default 60000)

await SimAgent.runUntil(predicate, { visual, maxSimMs })
  // predicate(state) returning bool â€” stop when true
  // e.g. SimAgent.runUntil(s => s.cc_phase === "cong-avoid")

// --- read ---
SimAgent.getState()                          // current state snapshot
SimAgent.getSummary()                        // computed summary of the run
SimAgent.getTrace()                          // array of 10 Hz samples
SimAgent.getEvents()                         // discrete events
SimAgent.getArtifact()                       // { run_id, version, config, samples, events, summary }

// --- replay a captured artifact, visually ---
await SimAgent.replay(artifact, { speed })

// --- meta ---
SimAgent.version                             // "1.0"
```

### Sample shape (10 Hz)

```js
{
  t_ms: 412,                       // sim time of this sample
  cwnd_seg: 1284,                  // current cwnd in segments
  cc_phase: "cong-avoid",          // "slow-start" | "cong-avoid"
  in_recovery: false,
  inflight_seg: 950,
  inflight_bytes: 1024576,
  q_depth_bytes: 384000,           // bottleneck queue depth
  q_drops_cumulative: 2,
  retransmits_cumulative: 0,
  acked_seg: 412,
  recv_seg: 489,
  goodput_KBms: 14.8,
  goodput_Mbps: 121,
  rtt_ms: 60,                      // knob at moment of sample
  router_read_mbps: 10000,
  router_write_mbps: 1000,
  link_bw_mbps: 230,
  effective_drain_mbps: 230,
  mss_B: 1398,
  wmem_used_bytes: 1024576,
  rmem_used_bytes: 81084,
  advertised_rwnd_seg: 2950
}
```

### Event types

Emitted at most once per transition:

- `handshake_done`: the 3-way handshake completed and data may flow (only with `handshake: true`; fires at t â‰ˆ RTT)
- `first_loss`: first time the simulation experiences any loss (queue or background)
- `hystart_exit`: cwnd crossed BDP and slow-start switched to cong-avoid
- `recovery_enter` / `recovery_exit`: fast-recovery boundaries
- `rto`: a retransmission-timeout fired (severe loss path)
- `queue_drop_burst`: â‰¥5 drops in one sample interval (sign of bursty overflow)
- `bbr_state_change`: BBR transitioned between states (startup / drain / probe_bw / probe_rtt). Data includes the
  current `btlBw_Mbps` and `rtprop_ms`. Only emitted in `ccMode: "bbr"`.
- `finished`: sim completed (all payload acknowledged)

### Artifact shape

`getArtifact()` returns a fully self-contained record of the run:

```js
{
  run_id: "uuid",
  version: "1.0",
  config: { /* every knob value at run time */ },
  samples: [ /* 10 Hz snapshots */ ],
  events: [ /* discrete events */ ],
  summary: {
    completed: true,
    elapsed_sim_ms: 725,
    acked_segments: 7182,
    acked_pct: 100,
    goodput_KBms: 14.46,
    goodput_Mbps: 115.7,
    cwnd_min: 10, cwnd_max: 713, cwnd_final: 713,
    queue_drops_total: 0,
    retransmits_total: 0,
    wmem_peak_bytes: 4194000,
    rmem_peak_bytes: 215292,
    first_loss_t_ms: null,
    hystart_exit_t_ms: 502.5
  }
}
```

## Examples

### Run a scenario deterministically

```js
SimAgent.applyPreset("cold-iw10");
await SimAgent.runToCompletion({ visual: false });
SimAgent.getSummary();
// â†’ { completed: true, elapsed_sim_ms: 353, goodput_Mbps: ..., ... }
```

### Sweep a knob, watch each run

```js
const results = [];
for (const queueKB of [50, 200, 500, 1500, 4500]) {
  SimAgent.applyPreset("warm-iw3500-rwnd-warm");
  SimAgent.configure({ queueKB, simSpeed: 0.3 });
  await SimAgent.runToCompletion({ visual: true });
  results.push({ queueKB, ...SimAgent.getSummary() });
}
```

### Stop on first loss

```js
SimAgent.applyPreset("warm-iw3500-rwnd-warm");
await SimAgent.runUntil(s => s.q_drops_cumulative > 0 || s.retransmits_cumulative > 0);
SimAgent.getState();
// â†’ state at first loss
```

### Compare the blog presets

```js
const traces = {};
for (const preset of ["cold-iw10", "warm-iw10", "warm-iw3500-rwnd-warm"]) {
  SimAgent.applyPreset(preset);
  await SimAgent.runToCompletion({ visual: false });
  traces[preset] = SimAgent.getArtifact();
}
```

## Driving the page from outside the browser

The agent API lives in the page's JavaScript context. To reach it from external automation:

- **Playwright / Puppeteer**: `page.evaluate(async () => { ... SimAgent.something() })` works directly because
  `page.evaluate` runs in page world. See `examples/agent-driver.js`.
- Browser automation that runs scripts in an *isolated world* (e.g. AppleScript's `execute javascript`) can't see
  `window.SimAgent` directly. Workaround: inject a `<script>` element into the page world, have it write results to
  `document.title`, then poll the title from outside.

## Fidelity notes

This is a teaching model, not a packet-accurate emulator. `link_bw` represents path capacity, router write represents
router egress capacity, and the queue drains at `min(link_bw, router_write)`. Router read controls how fast bursts enter
that bottleneck queue. Cubic sends are ACK-clocked and unpaced by default â€” a cwnd-worth of segments can hit the queue up to the
router-read rate, which makes shallow-buffer scenarios harsher than a fully paced sender. Optional Cubic pacing spaces
sends at `cwnd_bytes / RTT Ã— cubicPacingGain` while keeping Cubic's cwnd and loss-response logic. Hystart exits
slow-start at exactly BDP and switches to linear AIMD growth until the first loss anchors the
cubic curve. The RTO model is simplified (uses a fixed minimum timeout floor, no SRTT variance tracking or
exponential backoff; restarts from a one-packet loss window). BBR repairs losses
quickly (SACK-style: an unACKed segment with 3 ACKed segments above it is retransmitted immediately, plus dup-ACK
fast retransmit) but ignores them for rate control, as BBRv1 largely did â€” its window/pacing come from the
BtlBw/RTprop model only, so random loss barely dents BBR goodput while it cripples cubic. The `rto` event is not
emitted in BBR mode.

## Constraints (things you cannot do as an agent)

- Read the rendered canvas pixels â€” only structured state via the API.
- Modify the page DOM/CSS beyond what the agent API allows.
- Persist state across page reloads.
- Run multiple simulations in parallel from a single tab.

## Source

The main source files:

- `sim-core.js` â€” pure simulation model (sim state factory, step function, presets). No DOM. Usable in Node or browser.
- `sim-runner.js` â€” shared runner. Owns the RAF loop (browser) and the tight deterministic loop (Node). Captures 10 Hz
  samples and discrete events.
- `sim-agent-api.js` â€” exposes `window.SimAgent`.
- `tcp-inflight.html` â€” the page. Visualization, controls, hooks into the runner.
- `index.html` â€” local-root iframe wrapper; production `/` is rewritten directly to `tcp-inflight.html`.
- `sim-cli.js` â€” CLI usage (`node sim-cli.js --preset cold-iw10`).

Plus `examples/agent-driver.js`, a Puppeteer script that drives the page end to end.
